title: Cat or Kitten Chewed on Electrical Cord: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your kitten chewed an electrical cord, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn the risks of electric shock and why immediate vet care is vital.
content_html:
TL;DR: Electrical cord injuries are life-threatening emergencies that can cause delayed lung damage and heart issues; seek immediate veterinary care even if your cat appears normal or only has minor mouth burns.

What happens when a cat experiences an electrical cord injury?

An electrical cord injury occurs when a pet, often a curious kitten, bites through the protective insulation of a live wire. This creates an immediate circuit through the animal's body. While the most visible signs are often burns on the tongue or gums, the primary danger lies in the internal damage caused by the current, which can affect the heart's rhythm and cause fluid accumulation in the lungs.

Is an electrical cord injury a veterinary emergency?

  • High Urgency: This is a life-threatening situation; contact an emergency veterinarian immediately for an evaluation.
  • Delayed Symptoms: Systemic effects can be delayed; fluid in the lungs (pulmonary edema) may develop several hours after the event.
  • Potential for Respiratory Distress: Even if your cat appears normal or only has minor mouth redness, they are at risk for sudden, severe breathing difficulties.

How can a photo of the scene help with veterinary triage?

  • Safety Precaution: Only take photos after ensuring the device is unplugged and there is no further risk of shock to you or your pet.
  • Voltage Assessment: A photo of the damaged cord helps the veterinary team estimate the potential voltage and current involved in the injury.
  • Tissue Damage Evaluation: Providing a clear photo of burns inside the mouth allows the vet to perform an initial assessment of tissue damage while you are in transit.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Chewing on electrical cords is most frequently observed in kittens. Clinical signs include hesitation to eat or drink, drooling, and resentment of handling. Oral examination may reveal a linear scar across the dorsum of the tongue, outlining the path of the electric cord. One or both lip commissures may have a scar or wound, and the adjacent carnassial teeth may be discolored, potentially requiring endodontic treatment. Systemic involvement, such as noncardiogenic pulmonary edema, can be life-threatening and requires evaluation and treatment.

Chapter: Dentistry

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 366)

faqs.question: What are the immediate symptoms of electric shock in cats?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include burns or charring on the lips and tongue, excessive drooling, rapid breathing, and general distress or hiding.

faqs.question: Can I wait a few hours to see if my cat acts differently?
faqs.answer: No. The most dangerous complications, such as fluid in the lungs or heart arrhythmias, are often delayed. Professional monitoring is required to ensure the cat remains stable.

faqs.question: What should I do first if I see my cat chewing a cord?
faqs.answer: Do not touch the cat or the cord until you have safely turned off the power at the source or unplugged the device to avoid being shocked yourself. Once the area is safe, transport your cat to a vet.
